Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a set routine most of the time. I'd study in bursts when I felt like it or when anxiety about falling behind would kick in. With 15 online courses going on, I kind of just floated between them based on what felt most urgent. I'd listen to lectures while doing other things around the house, which worked for me since I learn better by hearing things anyway. Some weeks I'd put in a solid 11 hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd go weeks without touching certain courses, then cram when deadlines loomed. My extracurriculars and just life stuff at 28 means I have competing priorities that younger students might not have. I'm not proud of the inconsistency, but it's how I've been surviving these courses.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification that scared me into action or this nagging anxiety that I was falling behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, scroll around nervously, and then log out without doing much. The anxiety would push me to check, but then I wouldn't always follow through with actual work.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got into a rhythm where logging in felt natural or automatic. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, but it never stuck. I think having so many courses made it hard to build habits with any single one.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I watched over three hours of video content because I could listen while doing other things and the information actually stuck. Discussion posts were also useful when people asked questions I hadn't thought of—seeing different perspectives helped concepts click.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely actually read them. Written content just doesn't engage me the same way audio does. I also skipped most optional activities because I was already overwhelmed with required work across all my courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to exams or assignments, or if it was in a format that suited how I learn. If I could listen to it while multitasking, even better. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With my attendance already low and assignments piling up across 15 courses, optional stuff was the first thing to go. Required activities carried weight because they affected my grade, so even when I didn't want to do them, the anxiety about failing pushed me forward.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded tons of audio lectures and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos on similar topics when the course materials weren't clicking. My actual learning happened a lot outside the LMS, just in ways that were more convenient for my lifestyle.
Q10: There were many times I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times, taking mental notes, really processing the material—but the LMS would only show I accessed it once. Or I'd discuss concepts with friends who were in similar courses, working through problems verbally. None of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a lecture, watch it once online to register the view, then replay it offline several times. The LMS only captured that initial access. My actual engagement with the material was much deeper than what got recorded.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't face the courses. I'd feel paralyzed by how much I'd fallen behind, which made it harder to start anything. The mindfulness exercises actually helped during those times—they'd calm me down enough to at least do something small.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. At 28, I have responsibilities that drain me before I even get to studying. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS, stare at the screen, maybe watch a few minutes of video, and then give up. Competing responsibilities definitely meant the LMS got the leftover scraps of my energy.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. My body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made it worse—I'd feel so overwhelmed that starting felt impossible, so I'd avoid it entirely. It's a frustrating cycle because the avoidance just increased my anxiety, but I couldn't seem to break out of it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd ignore things until the last possible moment, then scramble. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure was the only thing that could override my avoidance patterns. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it gave me a temporary boost and made me more likely to engage with the next thing. But honestly, I often didn't check feedback promptly because I was anxious about what it might say. Good feedback helped; the fear of bad feedback made me avoid checking altogether sometimes.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across modules. I needed clearer priorities—what was essential versus nice-to-have. When everything seemed equally important, I'd freeze up and do nothing. A more streamlined structure would have helped me focus my limited energy.
Q19: Having audio options for content made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was the sheer volume of materials and the lack of clear guidance on what to prioritize. Also, discussion requirements felt forced sometimes—I'd post just to meet the requirement rather than because I had something meaningful to contribute.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in frequently out of anxiety, clicking around, watching bits of videos, but not actually completing assignments because of avoidance or overwhelm. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes being in the LMS is more about managing anxiety than actually working.
Q21: That's basically my situation. I performed reasonably well on exams because I actually learned the material through my own methods—listening to lectures repeatedly offline, discussing with others, using external resources. My LMS activity looks minimal, but my understanding of the content was solid enough to score well.
Q22: Logs can show patterns of when people access things and basic completion metrics. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background while doom-scrolling on their phone.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious, when concepts connect to things I care about, when learning feels meaningful rather than like a box to check. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time spent.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels, how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope, or how much learning happened in conversations and offline review. It wouldn't capture that I'm a 28-year-old juggling multiple life responsibilities, or that my engagement style is auditory and mobile rather than sitting at a computer clicking through modules.
